questions.stem: PATIENT: 33-year-old G4 | 2 PRIOR CS | 37 weeks | In ACTIVE LABOUR EXAM: 3cm cervical dilation | 40% effacement QUESTION: Next step? A 33-year-old G4 with 2 prior CS at 37 weeks, in active labour with 3cm dilation. NEXT STEP?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 2 PRIOR CS + IN ACTIVE LABOUR = EMERGENCY CS. Rationale: 2 previous CS means 2 uterine scars → very high risk of UTERINE RUPTURE with oxytocin augmentation or ongoing labour (particularly in active labour at 3cm). VBAC (trial of scar) may be considered after 1 CS but is generally NOT recommended after 2 CS (very high rupture risk). In active labour with 2 scars = deliver by CS now. App note: "Caesarean section is indicated in this patient since she has 2 previous CS; immediate C-section must be done because she is already in early labour."

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: 2 prior CS in active labour → do NOT allow labour to continue unmonitored without surgical intervention. Uterine rupture risk is high.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: OXYTOCIN AUGMENTATION IS CONTRAINDICATED with 2 prior CS -- dramatically increases uterine rupture risk. Even with 1 CS, oxytocin requires extremely careful monitoring.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: She is already in active labour at 37 weeks -- planning a 39-week CS is irrelevant. She needs intervention NOW.

questions.keyPoint: 2 PRIOR CS IN ACTIVE LABOUR = EMERGENCY CS. Do not attempt VBAC after 2 CS (very high

questions.stem: PATIENT: 33-year-old G1P0 (NULLIPAROUS) | 39 weeks PRESENTATION: BREECH | RUPTURED MEMBRANES | 2 cm cervical dilation USS: Estimated fetal weight = 4.5 kg (MACROSOMIC) HX: GESTATIONAL DIABETES MELLITUS NEXT STEP?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Macrosomic breech in a nulliparous woman with GDM and ruptured membranes = extremely high risk. Spontaneous breech delivery is not appropriate here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IOL with oxytocin for a breech at 39 weeks with macrosomic fetus and GDM = dangerous. Breech with ruptured membranes + macrosomia + nulliparous = CS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PGs are for cervical ripening -- inappropriate when membranes are already ruptured and in a breech macrosomic fetus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ECV is CONTRAINDICATED when membranes are already ruptured (cord prolapse risk increased) and fetus is macrosomic at term.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: INDICATIONS FOR EMERGENCY CS HERE: (1) BREECH presentation (2) NULLIPAROUS (no pelvic floor accommodation) (3) MACROSOMIA (EFW 4.5 kg) -- increases shoulder dystocia and birth trauma risk (4) RUPTURED MEMBRANES -- cord prolapse risk (5) GDM -- macrosomia with disproportionate fat deposition. Multiple converging factors demand immediate CS. App note: "Macrosomic fetus."

questions.keyPoint: BREECH + MACROSOMIA + NULLIPAROUS + RUPTURED MEMBRANES + GDM = EMERGENCY CS.

questions.stem: PATIENT: G3P2 | 40 weeks | Previous CS in obstetric history (not stated how many) QUESTION: Most important predictor of successful trial of labour (VBAC) A G3P2 at 40 weeks -- the most important PREDICTOR OF SUCCESS for TRIAL OF LABOR (VBAC) is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: erm pregnancy is standard -- not a specific predictor of VBAC success.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ot a strong predictor of VBAC success compared to previous VD.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: hort interpregnancy interval (<18-24 months) is associated with INCREASED rupture risk, but nterval >2 years is not the strongest VBAC success predictor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: he reason for previous CS matters somewhat (e.g., non-recurrent reason like fetal alpresentation = higher VBAC success) but is not the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT predictor.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: REVIOUS VAGINAL DELIVERY (especially previous VBAC) is the STRONGEST SINGLE REDICTOR of VBAC success. Women with a previous successful vaginal delivery (even before he CS) have VBAC success rates of 85-90% vs ~70-75% without previous VD. A previous uccessful VBAC has success rates >90% for subsequent VBAC. This is consistently the top redictor in all VBAC prediction models (Flamm, NICHD model).

questions.keyPoint: VBAC SUCCESS PREDICTORS: Previous vaginal delivery (strongest) | Previous VBAC | Non-recurrent

questions.stem: PATIENT: G2P1 | 39 weeks | In labour PROGRESS: Cervix changed from 1cm to 2cm over 3 HOURS QUESTION: Appropriate management? A G2P1 at 39 weeks whose cervix changed from 1cm to 2cm over 3 hours. APPROPRIATE CHOICE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: No indication for CS -- labour is progressing (1→2cm in 3h). This is not prolonged labour.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: o mention of CTG abnormality or fetal distress. FBS not indicated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: rogress of 1cm in 3 hours may be within normal for LATENT PHASE of labour in a multiparous oman. Oxytocin augmentation is not indicated for latent phase labour that is progressing, albeit lowly.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 2P1 at 39 weeks progressing from 1→2cm in 3h. This patient is in the LATENT PHASE 1-6cm). In the latent phase, labour can be slow and irregular -- this is normal. 1cm/3h is within cceptable latent phase progress. OBSERVATION is appropriate. Diagnosis of failure to rogress requires sustained inadequate progress in the ACTIVE PHASE (≥6cm) -- not in the atent phase at 1-2cm. App note confirms: "Observation."

questions.keyPoint: LATENT PHASE LABOUR (1-6cm): Progress can be slow and irregular -- up to 20h nulliparous, 14h

questions.stem: PATIENT: G4P3 | 38 weeks | IOL for pre-eclampsia EXAM: CERVIX: 3 cm dilated | -1 station | Cervical length 1 cm | MEDIUM consistency | Posterior position QUESTION: Calculate Bishop score Calculate the BISHOP SCORE: 3cm dilated | -1 station | 1cm long | Medium consistency | Posterior position

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Overestimates -- check scoring.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Underestimates.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Close but incorrect -- see full calculation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: BISHOP SCORE CALCULATION: Dilation 3cm=2pts | Effacement(length 1cm, ~66% effaced)=2pts | Station -1=1pt | Consistency medium=1pt | Position posterior=0pt. TOTAL=2+2+1+1+0=6? App shows 7. Alternate scoring: Dilation 3cm=2 | Cervical length 1cm (short)=2 | Station -1=1 | Consistency medium=1 | Position posterior=1. 2+2+1+1+1=7. Different sources use slightly different scoring scales for position and consistency. App answer = 7.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Severely underestimates.

questions.keyPoint: BISHOP SCORE: Dilation (0-3pts) + Effacement (0-3pts) + Station (-3 to +2 = 0-3pts) + Consistency

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: IOL first step with unfavourable cervix (Bishop <5) For WOMEN UNDERGOING IOL with a BISHOP SCORE of 5 OR LESS, which of the following is a commonly

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cervical application of C prostaglandin E2
choices.explanation: NFAVORABLE CERVIX (Bishop <6): FIRST STEP = CERVICAL RIPENING with ROSTAGLANDINS. Options: Dinoprostone (PGE2) cervical/vaginal gel or pessary (Propess) | isoprostol (PGE1) vaginal/oral. Mechanism: PGs cause cervical softening, effacement, and ilation (ripen the cervix) + mild uterine contractions. After ripening → reassess Bishop; if avorable → ARM + oxytocin.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ST assesses fetal wellbeing -- not a method of cervical ripening or IOL. Should be done EFORE starting IOL to confirm fetal wellbeing, not as the IOL method itself.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: OL can and should be initiated even with Bishop <5 when indicated (post-term, IUGR, etc.) -- but ith CERVICAL RIPENING first. IOL is not avoided; it is approached differently.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: xytocin requires a FAVORABLE CERVIX (Bishop ≥6-8). Using oxytocin on an unfavorable ervix → failed IOL, prolonged labour, hyperstimulation. PGs first, then oxytocin after ripening.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: S is not indicated simply because of an unfavorable cervix. PG cervical ripening resolves the nfavorable cervix in most cases.

questions.keyPoint: IOL SEQUENCE: Bishop <6: PGs (cervical ripening) FIRST → reassess → Bishop ≥6: ARM → oxytocin.

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Complications of epidural and spinal anesthesia in obstetrics A COMMON COMPLICATION of BOTH EPIDURAL AND SPINAL ANESTHESIA includes:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hyperventilation occurs in response to pain/anxiety in labour -- not from epidural/spinal. Epidural/spinal would REDUCE pain-driven hyperventilation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Tetanic contractions are caused by uterotonic overdose (oxytocin excess, ergot) -- not epidural/spinal.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Fetal bradycardia (not tachycardia) can occur post-epidural/spinal from maternal hypotension → uteroplacental insufficiency. Not fetal tachycardia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HYPOTENSION is the MOST COMMON complication of both epidural and spinal (intrathecal) anaesthesia. Mechanism: sympathetic blockade → vasodilation → reduced venous return → ↓ cardiac output → ↓ BP. Incidence: up to 30% with spinal (more profound), 10-15% with epidural. Prevention: IV fluid preload | Left lateral tilt (relieve aortocaval compression) | Vasopressors (phenylephrine preferred in obstetrics). Hypotension → fetal bradycardia → emergency.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Chorioamnionitis is from ascending bacterial infection -- epidural does not cause it. Prolonged epidural duration may correlate with chorioamnionitis due to prolonged labour, but is not a direct complication of the anaesthesia itself.

questions.keyPoint: EPIDURAL/SPINAL HYPOTENSION: Most common complication. Sympathetic blockade →

choices.label: A

APP NOTE: "GP (TPAL): T=Term births | P=Preterm births | A=Abortions | L=Living. From the note: G=2, P=1 (term VD at 39 wks), 0 (no preterm counted differently here), 1 (miscarriage at 9 wks), 1 (one living child). So G2P1011." The app calculation gives G2P1011 as the correct designation based on the specific history presented.

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Pelvic inlet measurements During an assessment of pelvic adequacy -- which of the following is ROUTINELY MEASURED?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The transverse diameter of the pelvic inlet (widest transverse) is measured on X-ray pelvimetry -- not routinely on clinical examination.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The TRUE CONJUGATE (AP diameter of pelvic inlet = ~11 cm) CANNOT be measured directly on clinical examination. It is the distance from sacral promontory to inner surface of symphysis pubis -- inaccessible to examining fingers.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The DIAGONAL CONJUGATE is the ONLY AP pelvic inlet diameter measured clinically on VE. It is the distance from the sacral promontory to the inferior edge of the symphysis pubis (measured by the examining finger). Normal: >12.5 cm. True conjugate is estimated: True conjugate = Diagonal conjugate - 1.5 cm. If DC ≥11.5-12 cm → true conjugate probably adequate.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Intertuberous diameter (ischial tuberosity width) is the PELVIC OUTLET diameter. Measured with a closed fist (Thoms pelviometer) -- assesses pelvic OUTLET, not inlet.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not a standard pelvimetric measurement.

questions.keyPoint: DIAGONAL CONJUGATE = ONLY routinely measured AP diameter of pelvic inlet. Measured clinically

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Correct statement about PPH Which of the following statements regarding POSTPARTUM HAEMORRHAGE is CORRECT?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bimanual uterine massage can be performed WITHOUT epidural anaesthesia. It is often done as an emergency procedure immediately postpartum.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxytocin stimulates upper AND lower segments
choices.explanation: OXYTOCIN primarily stimulates the UPPER UTERINE SEGMENT (fundus) -- the zone with the most oxytocin receptors. Lower segment has fewer receptors. This is why oxytocin causes fundal contraction (appropriate for PPH) and why it is NOT used in the first stage (would cause lower segment contraction = pain without progress).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: COMMONEST CAUSE OF PPH = UTERINE ATONY (70-80%). Perineal trauma (lacerations) is the second commonest cause.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atropine for uterine inversion tachycardia V
choices.explanation: UTERINE INVERSION causes VASOVAGAL RESPONSE → PROFOUND BRADYCARDIA and HYPOTENSION (not tachycardia). Treatment: ATROPINE for bradycardia + immediate uterine replacement (Johnson's maneuver or O'Sullivan hydrostatic method). The question says "tachycardia" -- this is incorrect; it should be bradycardia. igh-Yield Explanation

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: GOMETRINE causes powerful vasoconstriction (alpha-adrenergic + 5-HT effects) → gnificantly RAISES BLOOD PRESSURE. Absolutely contraindicated in: pre-eclampsia | lampsia | essential hypertension | cardiac disease. Using ergometrine in hypertension → pertensive crisis, stroke, CVA.

questions.keyPoint: ERGOMETRINE: Powerfully vasoconstrictive → CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension (any form). Also

questions.stem: PATIENT: G2P1 | 39 weeks | In labour PRESENTATION: Dark blood + mucus mixed with some contractions QUESTION: Most likely etiology of bleeding? Most likely ETIOLOGY of her bleeding?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Abruption: dark painful bleeding + rigid uterus + fetal distress. Not associated with mucus. This patient has contractions and mucus-mixed bleeding = normal labour process.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cervical laceration causes bright red bleeding, typically in active labour or after delivery. Not associated with mucus at onset of labour.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: DARK BLOOD + MUCUS + CONTRACTIONS in a term patient = BLOODY SHOW. The cervical mucus plug (operculum) is dislodged as the cervix begins to dilate → mixed with blood from small capillary rupture in the cervix → appears as dark blood-tinged mucus. This is a NORMAL SIGN that labour is imminent. Completely normal and expected.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Praevia: painless, bright red bleeding (no mucus). No contractions initially.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vasa praevia: bright red FETAL blood + immediate fetal distress at membrane rupture. Not mucus-stained dark blood.

questions.keyPoint: BLOODY SHOW: Normal sign of early labour. Dark blood-tinged MUCUS from cervical plug

questions.stem: PATIENT: G1P0 | 41 weeks | CERVIX: 1cm, 0% effaced, FIRM, POSTERIOR, -3 station, vertex QUESTION: Best next step in management? G1P0 at 41 weeks with cervix 1cm/0% effaced/firm/posterior/-3. BEST NEXT STEP?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CS is not indicated for a post-term pregnancy with unfavorable cervix when fetal wellbeing has not been assessed. IOL with cervical ripening should be offered first.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Normal kick count reference is >10 in 2 hours or >4 in 1 hour -- not 20 in 3h. And kick counts alone are not sufficient post-41-week surveillance.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: At 43 weeks, perinatal mortality is very high. NICE recommends IOL no later than 42 weeks. Waiting until 43 weeks is DANGEROUS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Send for IOL -- favorable bishop score Ver
choices.explanation: The bishop score described is VERY UNFAVORABLE (1cm/firm/posterior/-3 = Bishop ~1-2). Not favorable. IOL with such a cervix requires cervical ripening first. h-Yield Explanation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Order BPP for same or CORR next day
choices.explanation: 1 weeks with an unfavorable cervix, the patient needs: (1) FETAL SURVEILLANCE with BPP /or NST + AFI) to assess fetal wellbeing (2) Plan for cervical ripening/IOL (NICE: offer by 42 s). App note: "Either plan for CS or biophysical profile first to check fetal wellbeing, if not ressed then keep monitoring and provide prostaglandins for cervical ripening." BPP is the ect next step.

questions.keyPoint: 41 WEEKS + UNFAVORABLE CERVIX: (1) BPP/NST for fetal surveillance (2) Offer IOL with cervical

questions.stem: SHORT ANSWER SECTION -- Session 5 SA Q1 PATIENT: 28-year-old woman | First prenatal visit | Uterus palpable MIDPOINT between symphysis pubis and umbilicus The uterus palpable at the MIDPOINT between the symphysis pubis and umbilicus corresponds to what

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: At 12 weeks, the uterus is just palpable ABOVE the symphysis pubis (at the pubic hairline). Not yet at midpoint between symphysis and umbilicus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: At 6 weeks, the uterus is not palpable abdominally -- remains within the pelvis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: At 20 weeks, the fundal height reaches the UMBILICUS. This is a key landmark ("20 weeks = at umbilicus").

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: FUNDAL HEIGHT LANDMARKS: 12 weeks = at symphysis pubis | 16 weeks = MIDWAY between symphysis and umbilicus | 20 weeks = at umbilicus | 24 weeks = midway between umbilicus and xiphoid | 28 weeks = 3 finger-breadths above umbilicus | 36 weeks = xiphisternum | 40 weeks = slightly below xiphoid (lightening). App note: "At 16 weeks gestation, the fundus of the uterus can be palpated at the midpoint between the umbilicus and the pubic symphysis."

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: At 25 weeks, the fundus is above the umbilicus (approximately 25cm above symphysis by fundal-symphysis measurement = Bartholomew rule).

questions.keyPoint: FUNDAL HEIGHT LANDMARKS: 12=symphysis | 16=halfway between symphysis and umbilicus |

questions.stem: SHORT ANSWER -- Session 5 SA Q2 QUESTION: Which is NOT an indication for VACUUM-ASSISTED VAGINAL DELIVERY? Which of the following is NOT an indication for VACUUM ASSISTED VAGINAL DELIVERY?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FETAL MACROSOMIA is a RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATION to vacuum-assisted delivery (not an indication). Large babies risk: (1) Failed vacuum (scalp cup avulsion) (2) SHOULDER DYSTOCIA after head delivery (3) Increased cephalohematoma/intracranial haemorrhage risk. In suspected macrosomia, CS may be safer than attempted vacuum.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Non-reassuring CTG in 2nd stage = expedite delivery. Vacuum can achieve this if head is engaged and cervix fully dilated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Failure of fetal head to descend despite adequate pushing = instrumental delivery or CS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Women with cardiac disease, severe hypertension, or exhaustion: shortening 2nd stage with vacuum reduces maternal exertion and Valsalva strain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Fetal distress in 2nd stage = deliver urgently. Vacuum (if head engaged, fully dilated) is an appropriate option.

questions.keyPoint: VACUUM DELIVERY INDICATIONS: Prolonged 2nd stage | Non-reassuring FHR in 2nd stage | Maternal

questions.stem: SHORT ANSWER -- Session 5 SA Q3 QUESTION: Which condition is an INDICATION FOR INDUCTION OF LABOUR? INDUCTION OF LABOUR is INDICATED in which of the following conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: UTI is treated with antibiotics. It is not an indication for IOL -- antibiotics resolve the infection without need for delivery.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION / PRE-ECLAMPSIA = INDICATION FOR IOL. Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy worsen with time → delivery is the only definitive treatment. At 37 weeks or beyond with PIH: IOL is indicated. Severe PE → deliver regardless of gestation. PIH with end-organ damage → deliver. The placenta is the cause; delivery resolves it.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SPD is managed conservatively (physio, pain relief, mobility aids). Not an indication for IOL.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Grade 3 (major) praevia = ABSOLUTE INDICATION FOR CS -- NOT IOL. IOL is absolutely contraindicated in major praevia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: 2 prior CS = indication for ELECTIVE CS, not IOL. IOL with 2 CS scars carries unacceptably high uterine rupture risk.

questions.keyPoint: IOL INDICATIONS: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (PIH/pre-eclampsia) | Post-term pregnancy |

questions.stem: SHORT ANSWER -- Session 5 SA Q4 QUESTION: Which statement about IOL is TRUE? About INDUCTION OF LABOUR, which of the following is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BISHOP SCORE maximum = 13 (5 parameters × max 3 points each, though position/consistency max 2 each). App note: "Total bishop score is 13." Not 10.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: COMBINING medical (PGs) and surgical (ARM) methods IS beneficial -- sequential use achieves higher IOL success rates than either alone. ARM after PG ripening + then oxytocin = standard augmentation protocol.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: INDUCTION = initiating labour in a woman NOT in labour (i.e., starting labour). AUGMENTATION = stimulating labour that has already started but is progressing slowly. Different clinical contexts.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The BISHOP SCORE was originally developed to PREDICT THE LIKELIHOOD OF SUCCESSFUL VAGINAL DELIVERY when IOL is planned. A higher Bishop score predicts shorter IOL-to-delivery interval and higher vaginal delivery rates. Bishop <6 = unfavorable (higher IOL failure/CS rate). Bishop ≥8 = favorable (lower CS rate). App note: "Pre-induction Bishop scoring can predict success of induction."

questions.keyPoint: BISHOP SCORE: Developed by Edward Bishop (1964) to predict IOL success. Max = 13. ≥8 = favorable.

questions.stem: SHORT ANSWER -- Session 5 SA Q5 CLINICAL SCENARIO: Prolonged pregnancy -- fetal risks You are receiving a G3P2A0 pregnant lady at 40 weeks for IOL counselling. You are telling her that

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Stillbirth risk doubles after 41 weeks and increases steeply after 42 weeks -- one of the PRIMARY indications for IOL.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-term pregnancies have higher rates of meconium-stained liquor + meconium aspiration syndrome (fetal hypoxia → intestinal peristalsis → meconium passage → aspiration).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Post-term pregnancies have higher rates of macrosomia → shoulder dystocia is a well-recognised complication.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Continued fetal growth beyond term → macrosomia. Increased birth trauma, shoulder dystocia, CS risk.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The app marks neonatal jaundice as NOT a specific post-term complication. Post-term complications are primarily: macrosomia, meconium aspiration, oligohydramnios, cord compression, stillbirth, shoulder dystocia. Neonatal jaundice is a common NORMAL NEONATAL phenomenon (physiological jaundice) -- not specifically a post-term complication.

questions.keyPoint: POST-TERM PREGNANCY RISKS: Stillbirth | Meconium aspiration syndrome | Macrosomia | Shoulder

choices.label: A

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Same as B -- wrong drug for IOL.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: App marks D as correct. With Bishop score 7 (borderline favorable), PGs for further cervical preparation then oxytocin. Note: 1 prior CS makes oxytocin use require careful monitoring. PGs also require caution with prior CS (hyperstimulation risk). This is a pragmatic answer.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: While amniotomy (ARM) would be appropriate at Bishop 7, the app selects D.

questions.stem: PATIENT: G4P3 | 37 weeks | IOL for CHRONIC HYPERTENSION QUESTION: Which IOL method is NOT indicated for this patient? A G4P3 at 37 weeks admitted for IOL due to chronic hypertension. Which method of IOL is NOT indicated for

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Option D is the correct answer -- prostaglandins are the method that should be excluded.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ARM is safe in a woman with hypertension and is an appropriate step in IOL (after cervical ripening or if cervix already favorable).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mechanical methods (Foley catheter balloon, hygroscopic dilators) are safe alternatives for cervical ripening in hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PROSTAGLANDINS (especially PGF2-alpha like carboprost/dinoprost) cause VASOCONSTRICTION → can significantly RAISE BLOOD PRESSURE → dangerous in hypertensive women. PGE2 (dinoprostone) and PGE1 (misoprostol) are also relatively contraindicated or require extreme caution in hypertension because: (1) Prostaglandins modulate vascular tone (2) PGF2-alpha specifically causes intense vasoconstriction. ERGOMETRINE is also contraindicated in HTN. The note states: "use of prostaglandins could potentially exacerbate her condition due to the risk of enhancing vasoconstriction."

questions.keyPoint: PROSTAGLANDINS + HYPERTENSION: Use with caution or avoid (vasoconstriction risk). Safe IOL

questions.stem: PATIENT: G3P2A0 | 26 weeks | Cardiomegaly visible on CXR QUESTION: Best diagnostic test to RULE OUT CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE? A G3P2A0 at 26 weeks with cardiomegaly on CXR. Best test to RULE OUT congestive heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ECHO is the GOLD STANDARD for assessing cardiac structure and function in pregnancy. Provides: left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) | wall motion assessment | valve function | pericardial effusion | filling pressures. ECHO can definitively diagnose or exclude CHF (by measuring LVEF -- reduced in systolic heart failure). Safe in pregnancy (no radiation).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ECG assesses cardiac rhythm and conduction -- useful for arrhythmias and ischaemia. Does NOT assess cardiac pump function or fluid status -- cannot rule out CHF.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ABG assesses oxygenation and acid-base status -- can show hypoxaemia in severe pulmonary oedema but is not diagnostic of CHF and does not assess cardiac function.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CXR is a SCREENING tool (cardiomegaly already seen). It shows the result (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly) but does not provide definitive cardiac function assessment. ECHO needed to confirm diagnosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cardiac MRI is very accurate but: expensive | limited availability | not first-line in pregnancy (gadolinium contrast contraindicated in pregnancy; non-contrast MRI is safe but impractical as first-line).

questions.keyPoint: ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY = Gold standard for cardiac assessment in pregnancy. LVEF <40% = systolic

questions.stem: PATIENT: G2P1 | 30 weeks | OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS found on USS QUESTION: Most likely cause? A G2P1 at 30 weeks with OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS. Most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DM in pregnancy causes POLYHYDRAMNIOS (fetal hyperglycaemia → fetal polyuria + impaired swallowing). Not oligohydramnios.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: RENAL AGENESIS (Potter sequence/syndrome): complete absence of fetal kidneys → NO FETAL URINE PRODUCTION → SEVERE OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS. 95% of amniotic fluid in 2nd/3rd trimester is fetal urine. No kidneys = no urine = no amniotic fluid = oligohydramnios/anhydramnios. Results in: pulmonary hypoplasia (lungs require fluid for development) + Potter facies + limb deformities (from compression). Bilateral renal agenesis = lethal condition.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oesophageal atresia → fetus CANNOT SWALLOW amniotic fluid → fluid accumulates → POLYHYDRAMNIOS. Opposite of oligohydramnios.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Large choriangioma acts as AV fistula → can cause fetal high-output cardiac failure + POLYHYDRAMNIOS. Not oligohydramnios.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Duodenal atresia → impaired gut transit → POLYHYDRAMNIOS (double bubble sign on USS). Opposite of oligohydramnios.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BP 155/104 at 25 weeks = SEVERE HYPERTENSION in pregnancy. KEY CONCERN: HELLP SYNDROME (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) -- which presents with RUQ/epigastric pain (from liver capsule distension/haematoma). Also: severe pre-eclampsia with hepatic involvement. RUQ pain in hypertensive pregnant woman = EMERGENCY -- risk of hepatic rupture, eclampsia, imminent HELLP. This is the HIGHEST PRIORITY question.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Heartburn is common in pregnancy (GORD from uterine pressure) -- not a priority question in severe hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Common in pregnancy -- not specifically related to hypertensive emergency.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Low back pain is very common in pregnancy -- not a priority in this context.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Common pregnancy symptom -- not related to the hypertensive emergency.

questions.stem: PATIENT: 20-year-old G1P0 | 31 weeks | Being managed expectantly for proteinuric hypertension NEW DEVELOPMENT: Sudden abdominal pain + BP DROPPING + Fundal height RISING QUESTION: Most likely new development? Which is the MOST LIKELY new development?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HELLP causes: RUQ pain | Worsening BP (usually rising) | Thrombocytopenia | Elevated enzymes. Not associated with FALLING BP or RISING fundal height.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: THREE KEY SIGNS: (1) SUDDEN ABDOMINAL PAIN -- from retroplacental haematoma (2) FALLING BP + signs of SHOCK -- massive concealed haemorrhage (blood tracking between placenta and uterus = concealed abruption; does not always appear vaginally) (3) RISING FUNDAL HEIGHT -- expanding retroplacental haematoma causing uterus to enlarge (Couvelaire uterus). Hypertension IS a major risk factor for abruption. This combination is CLASSIC for severe concealed abruption. App note: "Sudden abdominal pain, drop in blood pressure suggest placental abruption (shock due to blood loss)."

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SROM causes: gush of clear fluid | no significant pain | no BP drop | no fundal height change.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Eclampsia: headache | visual disturbances | raised BP | convulsions. Not associated with falling BP or rising fundal height.

questions.stem: PATIENT: Same patient -- developed GENERALISED CONVULSIONS 6 hours after CS CONTEXT: Post-eclamptic convulsion in the immediate postoperative period QUESTION: ESSENTIAL management? Post-CS, she develops generalised convulsions. ESSENTIAL management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Catheter removal is NOT relevant to the acute management of an eclamptic convulsion. The Foley catheter is actually useful for monitoring urine output in eclampsia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ABC FIRST in any seizure. During a convulsion: the patient may obstruct their airway (tongue falling back, vomiting, secretions). AIRWAY MAINTENANCE is the most ESSENTIAL and IMMEDIATE first step in eclampsia management: (1) Call for help (2) LEFT LATERAL POSITION (prevents aortocaval compression + protects airway) (3) AIRWAY MAINTENANCE -- jaw thrust, suction, adjunct airway (4) O2 high flow (5) Then IV MgSO4 after airway secured.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MgSO4 dose for eclampsia: LOADING DOSE 4-6g IV over 15-20 min + MAINTENANCE 1g/h infusion. 1g alone is insufficient and not the loading dose protocol. Also: airway must be secured BEFORE giving MgSO4.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Methyldopa is an ANTIHYPERTENSIVE used CHRONICALLY in pregnancy (not for acute hypertensive crisis). For acute severe BP in eclampsia: IV labetalol | IV hydralazine | oral nifedipine. Not methyldopa in the acute setting.

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Peripartum cardiomyopathy -- which statement is TRUE? In PERIPARTUM CARDIOMYOPATHY, which of the following is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Permanent cardiac dysfunction in up to 20%
choices.explanation: PPCM prognosis: ~50-80% of women recover normal LV function within 6 months. However, ~20-50% develop PERSISTENT LV dysfunction (permanent LVEF <50%). This group has high morbidity: risk of arrhythmias, thromboembolic events, and death. A significant minority (up to 20% in some studies) never fully recover.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Minipill avoided with postpartum cardiac dysfunction
choices.explanation: The progestogen-only pill (POP/minipill) is actually PREFERRED CONTRACEPTION in women with cardiac disease -- it does NOT increase thrombotic risk unlike the COCP. COCP (oestrogen-containing) increases VTE risk → contraindicated in cardiac disease. Minipill is safe.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ACE INHIBITORS are FIRST-LINE treatment for PPCM (along with diuretics, beta-blockers, digoxin). They are CONTRAINDICATED during PREGNANCY but are the cornerstone of PPCM TREATMENT POSTPARTUM. This statement is false.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancy: ~50% -- not 85%. Higher risk in those with persistent LV dysfunction. Women with recovered LVEF still have ~21% recurrence; those with persistent dysfunction have ~50% deterioration.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Modern maternal mortality from PPCM in developed countries: approximately 3-8% in-hospital. 50% is a gross overestimate.

questions.keyPoint: PPCM: Occurs last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum. Definition: LVEF <45% + no prior

questions.stem: PATIENT: 42-year-old ASIAN woman | 4 weeks POST-DELIVERY SYMPTOMS: SHORTNESS OF BREATH lying flat (orthopnoea) EXAM: Apical impulse diffuse and displaced downward | Cardiomegaly on CXR RESPIRATORY: Fine basal crepitations | Vesicular breath sounds Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CLASSIC PPCM PRESENTATION: (1) TIMING: 4 weeks POST-DELIVERY (within the peripartum window of last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum) (2) ORTHOPNOEA: lying flat → increased venous return → LV cannot handle → pulmonary oedema → SOB (3) DIFFUSE DISPLACED APICAL IMPULSE: dilated LV → apex displaced laterally and downward (4) CARDIOMEGALY on CXR (5) BASAL CREPITATIONS: pulmonary oedema from LV failure. ASIAN women may have higher incidence (selenium deficiency postulated).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MR causes a PANSYSTOLIC murmur + volume overload + enlarged LV, but would not develop de novo 4 weeks postpartum in this clinical picture. PPCM is the primary diagnosis with this timeline.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MVP typically presents with a MID-SYSTOLIC CLICK + late systolic murmur. Usually mild and does not cause acute heart failure de novo 4 weeks postpartum.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HCM is a GENETIC condition (asymmetric septal hypertrophy). It causes DIASTOLIC dysfunction, dynamic LVOT obstruction. Does not present de novo 4 weeks postpartum with dilated/displaced apex.

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: ACUTE FATTY LIVER OF PREGNANCY (AFLP) -- which is TRUE? With regards to ACUTE FATTY LIVER OF PREGNANCY (AFLP):

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: AFLP causes HYPOGLYCAEMIA (low glucose) -- NOT hyperglycaemia. The liver is the primary source of glucose production (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis). In AFLP, massive hepatocyte dysfunction impairs glucose production → hypoglycaemia. Hypoglycaemia in the 3rd trimester = red flag for AFLP.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: AFLP was historically associated with >80% maternal mortality. With early recognition and delivery (the only definitive treatment), maternal mortality has dropped to <15%. However, it remains a potentially fatal condition if diagnosis and delivery are delayed. All of B, C, and D are TRUE per standard teaching.

choices.label: C
choices.text: May progress to fulminant hepatic failure
choices.explanation: AFLP can progress rapidly to: fulminant hepatic failure | encephalopathy | renal failure | DIC | death. Rapid progression is characteristic.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DIC is a recognised complication of AFLP -- liver failure impairs synthesis of clotting factors → coagulopathy. Also directly triggers DIC cascade.

questions.keyPoint: AFLP: Rare (<1:10,000) but potentially FATAL condition of late pregnancy. Features: 3rd trimester |

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Management of maternal cardiac condition in labour All the following are GENERAL PRINCIPLES for the management of MATERNAL CARDIAC CONDITION in

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Ergometrine (ergotamine) is contraindicated in cardiac disease: causes intense vasoconstriction → acute afterload increase → cardiac decompensation. AVOID in any cardiac condition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Boluses of oxytocin are safe to use
choices.explanation: RAPID IV BOLUS of oxytocin in cardiac disease is DANGEROUS: causes acute vasodilation → profound hypotension → reflex tachycardia → cardiac decompensation. SAFE approach: oxytocin as a SLOW IV INFUSION (never rapid bolus in cardiac patients). This statement is FALSE -- boluses are NOT safe.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Continuous CTG essential: maternal cardiac disease → uteroplacental insufficiency → fetal compromise risk. Continuous monitoring needed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Carefully administered epidural with gradual top-up reduces pain → reduces sympathetic stress response → reduces cardiac workload. BENEFICIAL in most cardiac conditions. Avoid in: severe aortic stenosis, IHSS with outflow obstruction (epidural-induced vasodilation worsens obstruction).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: BACTERIAL ENDOCARDITIS PROPHYLAXIS: required for high-risk cardiac lesions (prosthetic valves, previous endocarditis, complex CHD) during invasive procedures in labour (if sepsis or instrumented delivery). Amoxicillin or gentamicin depending on the lesion.

questions.keyPoint: CARDIAC DISEASE IN LABOUR: Avoid ergometrine | Oxytocin as SLOW INFUSION (no rapid boluses)

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Hysteroscopy distension media Which of the following are DISTENSION MEDIA used in HYSTEROSCOPY?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CARBON DIOXIDE (CO2) is used as a distension medium primarily in OFFICE/DIAGNOSTIC hysteroscopy. Advantages: excellent visualisation, rapid absorption, no fluid overload risk. Disadvantages: cannot be used with bleeding (bubbles obscure view), risk of gas embolism if absorbed rapidly, not suitable for operative hysteroscopy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Glycine 1.5% is a LIQUID distension medium used in OPERATIVE hysteroscopy (resectoscope procedures). Risk: DILUTIONAL HYPONATRAEMIA (glycine absorbed → low sodium → cerebral oedema/death). Being replaced by saline-based systems with bipolar energy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Air is CONTRAINDICATED as hysteroscopy medium -- risk of fatal air embolism. Never used.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Dextrose (5%) is occasionally used but not the primary medium. Glycine and saline are more common liquid media.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Distilled water causes haemolysis (hypotonic) -- not used as distension medium in hysteroscopy.

questions.keyPoint: HYSTEROSCOPY DISTENSION MEDIA: Diagnostic (office): CO2 (excellent visualisation, no bleeding) |

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: INTRAUTERINE DEATH (IUD) -- which statement is TRUE? Intrauterine fetal DEATH (IUD) -- which of the following statements is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gas gangrene not a complication of septic miscarriage
choices.explanation: Gas gangrene (Clostridium perfringens) IS a potentially fatal complication of septic abortion/miscarriage. The organism releases histotoxic exotoxins causing myonecrosis, gas formation, and systemic sepsis. This statement is FALSE.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coagulopathy after ~20 weeks of fetal retention
choices.explanation: RETAINED DEAD FETUS SYNDROME: When a dead fetus is retained in utero, thromboplastin (tissue factor) is gradually released from degenerating fetal and placental tissues into the maternal circulation. This activates the extrinsic coagulation pathway → progressive DIC. Clinically significant coagulopathy typically occurs when the fetus has been retained for approximately 4 WEEKS (some sources say after 3-5 weeks). The timing of "20 weeks" likely refers to the duration of retention -- though more standard teaching quotes ~4 weeks of retention.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: While these TORCH organisms CAN cause IUD, the MOST COMMON causes of IUD are: placental insufficiency/IUGR, pre-eclampsia, cord accidents, abruption, structural anomalies. TORCH infections cause a minority of IUD cases.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Misoprostol is HIGHLY EFFECTIVE for medical management of IUD -- success rates of 80-90% (not 40%). It is the standard pharmacological method.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DIC IS a well-recognised complication of retained dead fetus, severe abruption, AFLP, sepsis, and amniotic fluid embolism.

questions.keyPoint: RETAINED DEAD FETUS SYNDROME: Gradual release of fetal thromboplastin → DIC. Risk increases

questions.stem: PATIENT: 36-year-old | Antenatal clinic | Very concerned DATING: By date and early USS should be 38 weeks; NEW USS shows 34 weeks (4 weeks smaller) USS FINDING: MODERATE OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS QUESTION: Which cause of ASYMMETRIC IUGR is most likely? A 36-year-old with asymmetric IUGR and moderate oligohydramnios -- most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Early radiation causes SYMMETRIC IUGR (affects all organ development proportionally) or early pregnancy loss -- not asymmetric IUGR.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Most congenital malformations cause SYMMETRIC IUGR (global growth impairment). Asymmetric IUGR is from UTEROPLACENTAL INSUFFICIENCY.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ASYMMETRIC IUGR: Brain-sparing IUGR where head circumference is relatively preserved but abdominal circumference is reduced (liver small from glycogen depletion). CAUSE: UTEROPLACENTAL INSUFFICIENCY (placenta cannot deliver adequate nutrition). APS (antiphospholipid syndrome) causes: recurrent placental thrombosis → placental infarction → uteroplacental insufficiency → asymmetric IUGR. App note: "The main cause of asymmetric IUGR is placental insufficiency, and antiphospholipid syndrome is one of the causes of placental insufficiency."

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TORCH infections (toxoplasma, CMV, rubella) cause SYMMETRIC IUGR -- all organs affected equally because infection occurs during organogenesis/early fetal development.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Chromosomal anomalies (T21, T18, T13) cause SYMMETRIC IUGR from the outset (fundamental growth programming defect).

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Most frequent complication of CS hysterectomy Which of the following is the MOST FREQUENTLY ENCOUNTERED COMPLICATION of caesarean

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bladder and ureter injuries occur in CS hysterectomy (bladder 5-10%, ureter 1-2%) but are not the MOST COMMON complication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Need for transfusion of blood products
choices.explanation: MASSIVE HAEMORRHAGE requiring blood transfusion is the MOST COMMON complication of caesarean hysterectomy. The uterus receives ~500-700 mL/min of blood at term. Hysterectomy of a pregnant/immediately postpartum uterus (highly vascular) inevitably results in significant blood loss requiring transfusion in the majority of cases (reported in 50-80% of cases). Preoperative planning includes crossmatch of 6+ units and cell saver availability.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Endometritis cannot occur after hysterectomy (uterus removed). Post-hysterectomy pelvic infection can occur but is not the most common complication.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bowel injury occurs in some cases (especially percreta) but is less common than blood transfusion requirement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CS hysterectomy carries increased mortality risk, but death itself is not the most FREQUENT complication.

questions.keyPoint: CS HYSTERECTOMY: Most common complication = MASSIVE HAEMORRHAGE requiring transfusion.

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Indicators of fetal wellbeing after 24 weeks Which of the following is NOT an indicator of FETAL WELLBEING after 24 weeks?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fetal movements reflect fetal neurological function and energy substrate availability. Reduced FMs = potential fetal compromise. Mothers should be aware of normal fetal movement patterns. Kick counting is used as a surveillance tool.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Duplicate entry in question options -- same as A.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CTG assesses: fetal heart rate baseline | variability | accelerations | decelerations. Reactive CTG (2 accelerations in 20 min) = reassuring fetal wellbeing. Non-reactive = requires further assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Umbilical artery Doppler: measures uteroplacental resistance. Normal: forward end-diastolic flow. Abnormal: absent or reversed EDF = high risk of fetal compromise → deliver.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FETAL LIE (longitudinal vs oblique vs transverse) describes the ORIENTATION of the fetus within the uterus -- it is a POSITIONAL finding, not an assessment of fetal wellbeing. Fetal lie does not reflect fetal oxygenation, neurological status, or cardiovascular function.

questions.keyPoint: FETAL WELLBEING ASSESSMENTS: Fetal movements | CTG | BPP (breathing movements, tone, FMs,

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Best time to auscultate fetal heart To be more valuable, the fetal heart is BEST AUSCULTATED:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: During relaxation, the fetal heart rate is at its resting baseline -- this is useful but does not detect uterine contraction-related changes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: During contraction, it may be harder to auscultate clearly and does not allow assessment of post-contraction recovery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: FETAL HEART AUSCULTATION is most valuable IMMEDIATELY AFTER a uterine contraction. Reason: uterine contractions temporarily reduce uteroplacental blood flow. AFTER the contraction, if the fetal heart RECOVERS to its normal rate promptly = reassuring. If the fetal heart continues to slow AFTER the contraction (late deceleration pattern) = fetal hypoxia/distress. App note: "This timing is often considered the most valuable because it allows observation of the fetal heart rate recovery after a contraction. This can be crucial for assessing how well the fetus is tolerating labour."

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Before contraction establishes baseline; during contraction is impractical. The most diagnostic moment is AFTER.

questions.keyPoint: FETAL HEART AUSCULTATION post-contraction: Assesses recovery pattern. Failure to recover

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: CONGENITAL TOXOPLASMOSIS -- fetal manifestations CONGENITAL TOXOPLASMOSIS INFECTION is associated with what FETAL MANIFESTATIONS?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This mixed description most closely describes CMV (cytomegalovirus) -- the most common intrauterine infection causing periventricular calcifications, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, jaundice. The periventricular distribution of calcifications is key for CMV.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: THE CLASSIC TOXOPLASMOSIS TRIAD: (1) CHORIORETINITIS (most common manifestation -- inflammatory lesions of retina/choroid → poor vision/blindness) (2) INTRACRANIAL CALCIFICATIONS (scattered/diffuse -- distinguish from CMV which is PERIVENTRICULAR) (3) HYDROCEPHALUS (cerebral aqueduct obstruction by inflammation/calcification). May also include: convulsions, psychomotor retardation, fever, rash, hepatosplenomegaly.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: These are features of CONGENITAL SYPHILIS: Hutchinson triad (interstitial keratitis + nerve deafness + Hutchinson teeth) + saddle nose + saber shins + perioral rhagades. NOT toxoplasmosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This describes CONGENITAL LISTERIOSIS (Listeria monocytogenes) -- causes granulomatosis infantiseptica with granulomatous lesions throughout organs.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This describes CONGENITAL RUBELLA SYNDROME: classic triad = Cataracts + Cardiac defects (PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis) + Deafness (sensorineural). +/- Blueberry muffin rash, IUGR.

questions.keyPoint: TOXOPLASMOSIS TRIAD: Chorioretinitis + Intracranial calcifications (DIFFUSE/scattered) +

questions.stem: PATIENT: 22-year-old | Septate uterus diagnosed QUESTION: Highest risk for which pregnancy complication? You see a 22-year-old who has been diagnosed with a SEPTATE UTERUS. At highest risk for which

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Septate uterus CAN cause 1st trimester loss (implantation on avascular septum → poor placentation). However, the 2nd trimester is the most characteristic timing.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Not specifically associated with septate uterus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Septate uterus is a Mullerian duct anomaly. Renal anomalies are associated with Müllerian anomalies generally, but not the HIGHEST RISK for this specific complication in septate uterus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SEPTATE UTERUS = MOST COMMON uterine anomaly (50% of uterine anomalies). HIGHEST RISK: Mid-trimester (second-trimester) loss. Mechanism: (1) Avascular fibrous septum -- poor implantation site if placenta attaches to septum (2) Cervical incompetence -- septum may compromise cervical function (3) Restricted uterine cavity -- as pregnancy grows, the septum restricts expansion, causing preterm labour or fetal loss. Also: preterm labour, breech presentation, CS rates all increased.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not specifically the highest risk complication for septate uterus.

questions.keyPoint: SEPTATE UTERUS: Most common Mullerian anomaly. Worst reproductive outcome of all anomalies.

questions.stem: PATIENT: 41-year-old G4P4 | 5 weeks post-ELECTIVE CS for placenta accreta EVENT: SUDDEN COLLAPSE HX: Hospitalised for >5 weeks | Prolonged immobility | Recent major surgery Most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Endometritis cannot occur after CS hysterectomy (uterus removed). Septic shock 5 weeks post-op is possible but would have a prodrome of fever, infection signs -- not sudden collapse.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Same as A -- duplicate distractor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SUDDEN COLLAPSE 5 WEEKS AFTER MAJOR SURGERY in a woman with prolonged immobility = PULMONARY EMBOLISM (PE) until proven otherwise. RISK FACTORS PRESENT: (1) Prolonged immobility (>5 weeks hospitalised) (2) Major pelvic surgery (3) Postpartum state (pregnancy = hypercoagulable) (4) Multiparity. PE classically presents with: sudden collapse | hypoxia | chest pain | haemoptysis | tachycardia | hypotension. App note: "Prolonged immobility and recent surgery."

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Eclampsia occurs in the context of hypertensive disease in pregnancy or postpartum. No mention of HTN, proteinuria, or pre-eclampsia signs. Eclampsia at 5 weeks postpartum would be very unusual (most occur within 4 weeks).

questions.keyPoint: SUDDEN COLLAPSE POST-MAJOR SURGERY + PROLONGED IMMOBILITY = PULMONARY

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: POLYCYSTIC OVARIAN SYNDROME (PCOS) -- which statement is TRUE? Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding POLYCYSTIC OVARIAN SYNDROME (PCOS)?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: PCOS is characterised by HYPERGONADOTROPIC OVARIAN DYSFUNCTION -- actually PCOS shows: normal/elevated LH | elevated LH:FSH ratio (>2:1) | normal or low FSH | elevated androgens. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low FSH, low LH) is caused by: hypothalamic amenorrhoea (low weight, stress, exercise) -- NOT PCOS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most common cause of chronic anovulation
choices.explanation: PCOS is the MOST COMMON CAUSE OF ANOVULATORY INFERTILITY, accounting for ~75% of anovulatory infertility. Mechanism: elevated insulin → increased ovarian androgen production → follicle arrest at early antral stage → no dominant follicle → no ovulation → anovulation. Also raises LH → further androgen stimulation → LH:FSH ratio elevated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PCOS more commonly presents with OLIGOMENORRHOEA (infrequent periods) -- NOT complete amenorrhoea. Amenorrhoea occurs but is less common. The periods are irregular (occurring every 35-90 days) rather than completely absent.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PCOS: oestrogen is NOT depleted. In fact, there is PERIPHERAL OESTROGEN from excess androgen aromatisation in adipose tissue. Chronic oestrogen exposure without progesterone (no ovulation) → endometrial hyperplasia risk. Depressed oestradiol characterises PREMATURE OVARIAN INSUFFICIENCY (POI) -- not PCOS.

questions.keyPoint: PCOS = MOST COMMON CAUSE OF ANOVULATORY INFERTILITY (75% of cases). Rotterdam criteria

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Ovarian drilling procedure (Image shown in app -- appears to show laparoscopic ovarian drilling instrument) What is the PRIMARY GOAL of the procedure depicted? (Ovarian drilling)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LAPAROSCOPIC OVARIAN DRILLING (LOD): A surgical treatment for PCOS-related anovulatory infertility when ovulation induction with clomiphene has FAILED. Mechanism: diathermy or laser punctures (4-20) destroy androgen-producing stromal tissue → lowers serum androgens + LH → restores FSH sensitivity → follicle development → ovulation. SUCCESS: ~80% achieve ovulation post-LOD.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: While LOD reduces androgens (which may slightly help hirsutism), this is NOT the primary goal. Primary goal = restore ovulation for infertility.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LOD destroys ANDROGEN-PRODUCING STROMAL TISSUE -- not all antral follicles. Destroying all follicles would cause premature ovarian failure. LOD is targeted and controlled.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LOD does NOT cause menopause. It is a FERTILITY-PRESERVING procedure that restores normal ovarian function by reducing excessive androgen production.

questions.keyPoint: OVARIAN DRILLING: Laparoscopic | Electrocautery/laser punctures in ovarian stroma | Destroys

questions.stem: CLINICAL QUESTION: Fetal tachycardia -- what is NOT associated with it? FETAL TACHYCARDIA can NOT be associated with:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: INFECTION (chorioamnionitis) → maternal/fetal fever → increased heart rate. Fetal tachycardia >160 bpm with maternal fever = chorioamnionitis until proven otherwise.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Beta-2 agonists (ritodrine, salbutamol, terbutaline) cross the placenta → stimulate fetal cardiac beta receptors → fetal tachycardia. Well-recognised side effect of tocolytic therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ACUTE hypoxia → catecholamine release → initial tachycardia (compensatory). Prolonged hypoxia → bradycardia (decompensation). Early hypoxia response = tachycardia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: During FETAL SLEEP STATES, the fetal heart rate DECREASES (lower baseline, reduced variability, no accelerations). This can mimic a non-reactive CTG. Fetal sleep does NOT cause tachycardia -- it causes relative bradycardia/reduced rate variability.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FETAL ANAEMIA (Rh disease, parvovirus B19) → reduced O2-carrying capacity → hypoxia → compensatory tachycardia. Also: hyperdynamic circulation in anaemia = tachycardia.

questions.keyPoint: FETAL TACHYCARDIA (>160 bpm) CAUSES: Maternal fever/infection | Betamimetics | Acute hypoxia |

questions.stem: PATIENT: 30-year-old | IDDM (Type 1 Diabetes) | 35 weeks HX: No fetal movements felt for 3 DAYS QUESTION: Best method to CONFIRM FETAL VIABILITY? Best method to CONFIRM FETAL VIABILITY?

choices.label: B

Provides: fetal heart rate baseline + variability + accelerations (reactive = 2 accel ≥15 bpm for ≥15 sec in 20 min) + decelerations. In IDDM with reduced fetal movements: reactive CTG = reassuring. Non-reactive or decelerations = fetal compromise → urgent assessment/delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: X-ray does not assess fetal cardiac function, reactivity, or wellbeing. Exposes fetus to radiation unnecessarily. Not used for viability assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MRI provides anatomical assessment but is NOT used for acute fetal wellbeing/viability assessment. CTG is more appropriate, faster, and widely available.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Progesterone thickens cervical mucus → impenetrable to sperm. Primary POP mechanism.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Progesterone causes endometrial atrophy → unfavourable for implantation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Progesterone suppresses LH via negative feedback → prevents LH surge → prevents ovulation.

choices.explanation: Progesterone increases SHBG → more testosterone bound → lower FREE testosterone. Beneficial for acne/hirsutism.

questions.stem: CASE 1: 22-year-old woman | Irregular menses | ACNE | Increased facial hair BMI: 31 kg/m² | USS: Multiple small PERIPHERAL FOLLICLES in both ovaries Which hormone is MOST LIKELY ELEVATED in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: In PCOS, FSH is typically NORMAL or slightly low. The key hormonal abnormality is an ELEVATED LH:FSH ratio (>2:1 or >3:1).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PCOS is characterised by ELEVATED LH due to disrupted pulsatile GnRH release → preferential LH secretion. Elevated LH → increased ovarian androgen production (testosterone, androstenedione) → anovulation. LH:FSH ratio >2:1 is a classic PCOS finding.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oestradiol is not specifically elevated in PCOS -- it may be normal or slightly elevated from peripheral aromatisation of androgens. The primary hormonal defect is in LH/androgen axis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hyperprolactinaemia causes amenorrhoea and galactorrhoea -- NOT acne/hirsutism. Prolactin is typically normal in PCOS (but should be checked to exclude hyperprolactinaemia as a differential).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Elevated cortisol = Cushing syndrome -- causes central obesity, striae, buffalo hump, moon face. Can mimic PCOS but cortisol is not primarily elevated in PCOS.

questions.keyPoint: PCOS HORMONAL PROFILE: ↑ LH | ↑ LH:FSH ratio (>2:1) | ↑ Testosterone/androgens | Normal or ↑

questions.stem: CASE 1 continued: 22-year-old with PCOS | Irregular menses | No desire for pregnancy currently Most appropriate INITIAL TREATMENT for menstrual irregularity in PCOS?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Metformin (insulin sensitiser) is useful for metabolic features of PCOS and ovulation induction -- NOT the first-line for menstrual regulation when pregnancy is not desired.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: COCP is the FIRST-LINE treatment for menstrual irregularity in PCOS when the patient does not desire pregnancy. Benefits: (1) Regular withdrawal bleeds -- prevents endometrial hyperplasia from unopposed oestrogen (2) Anti-androgenic effect (oestrogen ↑ SHBG → ↓ free testosterone) → reduces acne and hirsutism (3) Contraception. Preferred formulations: COCP with cyproterone acetate (Dianette) or drospirenone for maximum anti-androgenic effect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Clomiphene (anti-oestrogen) is an OVULATION INDUCTION agent -- used when FERTILITY is desired. Not appropriate for menstrual regulation when contraception is wanted.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Weight loss is beneficial (5-10% loss can restore ovulation) but alone is not sufficient as initial pharmacological treatment for menstrual irregularity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cyclic progesterone can be used to induce regular withdrawal bleeds but does not provide contraception or treat the androgen excess/acne.

questions.stem: CASE 1 continued: PCOS patient Which LONG-TERM RISK is increased in women with PCOS?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Ovarian cancer risk is not specifically elevated in PCOS. (Endometrial cancer IS elevated due to unopposed oestrogen.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PCOS = strong independent risk factor for T2DM. 50-70% of women with PCOS have insulin resistance → over years, beta-cell exhaustion → impaired glucose tolerance → T2DM. Risk is 5-10x higher than age-matched non-PCOS women. Annual HbA1C/fasting glucose screening is recommended. Metabolic syndrome (HTN, dyslipidaemia, central obesity) also increased.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thyroid disease can be associated with menstrual irregularity but is not a long-term sequela of PCOS per se. Thyroid should be checked to exclude as a cause of symptoms.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PID is caused by ascending sexually transmitted infection -- not related to PCOS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cervical cancer is caused by HPV infection. Not related to PCOS.

questions.keyPoint: PCOS LONG-TERM RISKS: T2DM (5-10x) | Metabolic syndrome | Cardiovascular disease |

questions.stem: CASE 2: 29-year-old woman | GESTATION: 33 weeks PRESENTATION: PAINLESS vaginal bleeding | Uterus SOFT | Fetal heart sounds PRESENT Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CLASSIC PRESENTATION: PAINLESS bright red bleeding + SOFT non-tender uterus + normal FHR. At 33 weeks -- the lower uterine segment forms and the placenta is mechanically disrupted → painless bleeding. The uterus is SOFT (not rigid -- abruption would be rigid/woody). FHR normal (fetus not compromised at this stage).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Abruption = PAINFUL + RIGID/WOODY uterus + ± DIC. The soft uterus and painless bleeding strongly favour praevia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Vasa praevia = FETAL vessel rupture when membranes rupture → BRIGHT RED blood + IMMEDIATE fetal distress + fetal blood. No membrane rupture mentioned; FHS present and reassuring.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Uterine rupture = SUDDEN severe pain + maternal shock + fetal distress + scar history. Not this presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vaginal bleeding at 33 weeks is NEVER normal -- always investigate.

questions.keyPoint: PLACENTA PRAEVIA: PAINLESS + SOFT uterus + BRIGHT RED blood + NORMAL FHR = CLASSIC

questions.stem: CASE 2 continued: 29-year-old at 33 weeks | Confirmed PLACENTA PRAEVIA suspected What is the BEST DIAGNOSTIC TOOL?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TVUS is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosing and classifying placenta praevia. It provides precise measurement of the distance between the placental edge and the internal cervical os (most critical information for delivery planning). TVUS is SAFE -- the probe does not reach the cervix or placenta. Transabdominal USS (TAUS) is first line but TVUS gives superior visualisation of the lower placental edge. TVUS sensitivity for praevia: >95%.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MRI is used for staging PLACENTA ACCRETA SPECTRUM (not routine praevia diagnosis). Accurate but expensive, limited availability, not first-line for praevia diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CT uses ionising radiation -- inappropriate and unnecessary for obstetric diagnosis. USS is always preferred.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ionising radiation + no soft tissue detail for placenta. Inappropriate.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Speculum examination can be done (after USS excludes praevia) to assess cervical cause of bleeding. CANNOT diagnose praevia on its own. Also: speculum should NOT be performed before USS in APH.

questions.keyPoint: TVUS = Gold standard for placenta praevia diagnosis and classification. Safe, accurate, widely

questions.stem: CASE 2 continued: CONFIRMED MAJOR PLACENTA PRAEVIA at 33 weeks What is the important MANAGEMENT STEP?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MAJOR PRAEVIA MANAGEMENT: (1) NO DIGITAL VE -- catastrophic haemorrhage risk (2) Speculum examination only to assess bleeding source (after USS) (3) IV access + crossmatch (4) Corticosteroids at 33 weeks (before 34+6) → fetal lung maturity (5) Plan ELECTIVE CS at 36-37+6 weeks if no active bleeding. If major haemorrhage → emergency CS. Admit + monitor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in major praevia -- placenta covers birth canal → vaginal delivery = catastrophic haemorrhage for mother and fetus. NEVER attempt VD in major praevia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: At 33 weeks with active bleeding from confirmed praevia -- observation only is inadequate. IV access, crossmatch, steroids, hospital admission are all required.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NSAIDs have no role in APH management. They reduce prostaglandins and can worsen bleeding risk and impair platelet function.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No mention of diabetes. Insulin has no role here.

questions.keyPoint: MAJOR PRAEVIA MANAGEMENT: NO DIGITAL VE | USS first | Admit | IV access + crossmatch |

questions.stem: CASE 3: 32-year-old PRIMIGRAVIDA | GESTATION: 34 weeks SYMPTOMS: HEADACHE | BP: 160/100 mmHg | PROTEINURIA: 2+ on dipstick Name the MOST LIKELY DIAGNOSIS?

This patient has BP 160/100 (SEVERE) + proteinuria 2+ + headache (severe feature) = SEVERE PRE-ECLAMPSIA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Chronic HTN = hypertension diagnosed BEFORE 20 weeks or pre-existing. This is a primigravida at 34 weeks -- de novo → PE not chronic HTN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Eclampsia = seizures in context of PE. No seizures mentioned.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: BP 160/100 + proteinuria 2+ is NEVER normal in pregnancy.

choices.label: A

Removing the placenta (and fetus) is the only cure. All medical management (antihypertensives, MgSO4) is temporising -- to buy time for fetal maturity or to stabilise the mother before delivery. After delivery, BP and proteinuria typically normalise within days-weeks.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

choices.label: E

Up to 10% of PMB with ET ≥4mm have endometrial ncer; the risk increases with ET.

Mechanism: Excess dipose tissue → peripheral aromatisation of androgens to oestrone (an oestrogen) → NOPPOSED OESTROGEN stimulation of the endometrium → hyperplasia → carcinoma. Risk ncreases proportionally with BMI. Women with BMI >40 have ~6x increased endometrial cancer isk vs normal BMI.

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

Sensitivity for endometrial carcinoma: 80-90%. If Pipelle insufficient → hysteroscopy + D&C under anaesthesia.

questions.stem: CASE 4: 30-year-old woman | Inter-menstrual bleeding + SPOTTING for 3 months EXAM: No fever | Normal abdomen | No adnexal masses CERVIX: ERYTHEMATOUS + PURULENT DISCHARGE on examination Arabic note: ■■■ ■■cervicitis ■■■ ■■■■■■■ (approximately a cervicitis case) Most likely DIAGNOSIS?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CERVICITIS: Erythematous friable cervix + purulent discharge + intermenstrual bleeding + spotting. Caused by: Chlamydia trachomatis (most common) | Neisseria gonorrhoeae | HSV. Examination: inflamed, purulent cervix. NO fever/adnexal tenderness distinguishes from PID. The Arabic note on the source page confirms this is a cervicitis case.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Would present with PMB or irregular heavy periods. No cervical findings. Diagnosed by endometrial biopsy, not cervical examination.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CIN (cervical intraepithelial neoplasia) is pre-malignant. May present with post-coital bleeding but cervix may look normal or have white acetowhite lesions. No purulent discharge.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhoea + deep dyspareunia + pelvic pain. Not associated with erythematous cervix + purulent discharge.

questions.keyPoint: CERVICITIS: Erythematous purulent cervix + intermenstrual/post-coital bleeding = cervicitis. Most

questions.stem: CASE 4 CONTINUED: Cervicitis confirmed | Next step to CONFIRM the diagnosis Next step to CONFIRM the diagnosis:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: USS assesses pelvic organs. Cannot diagnose cervicitis -- which is a clinical + microbiological diagnosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MICROBIOLOGICAL CONFIRMATION of cervicitis: HIGH VAGINAL SWAB (HVS) + ENDOCERVICAL SWAB (ECS) sent for NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) for Chlamydia and Gonorrhoea. The endocervical swab specifically samples from the transformation zone where Chlamydia infects columnar epithelium. NAAT is >95% sensitive for both organisms.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Used for endometrial assessment. Not for cervicitis diagnosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Non-specific. Does not diagnose cervicitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The Arabic source note mentions colposcopy is NOT the best investigation here. Colposcopy is for CIN/cervical cancer screening -- not for infectious cervicitis diagnosis.

questions.keyPoint: CERVICITIS INVESTIGATION: HIGH VAGINAL SWAB + ENDOCERVICAL SWAB sent for NAAT
